name: ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN LABORATORY

topics.title: Design and Set Up of Inverting and Non-Inverting Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL308 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Set Up of Inverting and Non-Inverting Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, direct-coupled, differential input electronic voltage amplifier that uses external feedback components (resistors and capacitors) to control its response. When configured with negative feedback, it functions as a **linear amplifier** that can be tailored to perform precise mathematical operations such as amplification, summation, and integration.

In the context of this KTU lab experiment, the **μA741** (or equivalent) general-purpose op-amp is configured in two fundamental closed-loop topologies:

1. **Inverting Amplifier:** The input signal is applied to the inverting (-) terminal through an input resistor, while the non-inverting (+) terminal is grounded. The output is an amplified, phase-inverted (180° shifted) version of the input.
2. **Non-Inverting Amplifier:** The input signal is applied directly to the non-inverting (+) terminal. The output is an amplified, in-phase version of the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus Focus:** This experiment is **application-based** and tests the student's ability to translate theoretical gain equations into physical resistor values, build the circuit on a breadboard/PCB, and verify measured output against calculated theoretical values. The examiner expects a clear **Design → Setup → Measurement → Calculation** flow in your record.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Volume Knob" View

Imagine an op-amp as a **sophisticated microphone-to-speaker system** with a master volume knob. The op-amp itself is the amplifier core (the speaker system), but the actual "loudness" or gain is determined by the **two resistors** you place in the circuit — think of them as the volume knob settings.

- An **Inverting Amplifier** is like speaking into the *back* of a microphone: the sound comes out, but its polarity is reversed (phase-inverted), and the volume is controlled by the ratio of two specific knobs (Rf / Rin).
- A **Non-Inverting Amplifier** is like speaking into the *front* of the microphone: the sound is louder and remains faithful to the original phase. Here, the volume is controlled by a slightly different ratio (1 + Rf / Rin).

The **negative feedback** is like a self-correcting hand on the volume knob — it constantly compares the output to the input and adjusts the gain to keep the amplifier stable and predictable.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

- **Open-Loop Gain (A_OL):** > 200,000 (for 741) → practically treated as **infinite (∞)**
- **Input Impedance (Z_in):** ≈ 2 MΩ (741) → practically treated as **infinite (∞)**
- **Output Impedance (Z_out):** ≈ 75 Ω (741) → practically treated as **zero (0)**
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):** **1 MHz** for μA741
- **Slew Rate (SR):** **0.5 V/μs** for μA741
- **Supply Voltage:** ±15 V dual supply (standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in design:** The "infinite" and "zero" approximations are *the entire foundation* of the ideal op-amp assumption that makes the gain derivation trivially simple. In the KTU lab record, you must explicitly state these assumptions before any derivation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis

These two rules are derived directly from the infinite input impedance and infinite open-loop gain assumptions. They simplify all op-amp circuit analysis.

- **Rule 1 — Virtual Short:** The voltage difference between the two input terminals is zero.
$$V_+ \;=\; V_-$$
- **Rule 2 — Virtual Open:** No current flows into the op-amp's input terminals.
$$I_+ \;=\; I_- \;=\; 0 \;A$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **Inverting Amplifier**, the inverting terminal is at "virtual ground" because the non-inverting terminal is physically grounded and Rule 1 forces the inverting terminal to follow it. Hence $V_- \;=\; 0$ V.

### 2.2 Topology 1: Inverting Amplifier — Step-by-Step Logic

**Signal Flow:**
1. Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting (-) terminal via resistor $R_1$.
2. Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output back to the inverting (-) terminal.
3. Non-inverting (+) terminal is connected directly to **GND**.
4. Negative feedback forces the inverting terminal to a "virtual ground" (0 V).

**Closed-Loop Voltage Gain:**
$$A_v \;=\; \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \;=\; -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output.

**Input Impedance of the Inverting Amplifier:**
$$Z_{in} \;=\; R_1$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a critical KTU exam point: the input impedance of the inverting amplifier is **NOT** the op-amp's internal input impedance — it is solely determined by the external input resistor $R_1$. Choosing a small $R_1$ results in poor input impedance.

### 2.3 Topology 2: Non-Inverting Amplifier — Step-by-Step Logic

**Signal Flow:**
1. Input $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the non-inverting (+) terminal.
2. A voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_f$ (to output) feeds back a fraction of the output to the inverting (-) terminal.
3. Negative feedback forces $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$.

**Closed-Loop Voltage Gain:**
$$A_v \;=\; \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \;=\; 1 \;+\; \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

The output is **in-phase** with the input (no sign inversion).

**Input Impedance of the Non-Inverting Amplifier:**
$$Z_{in} \;\approx\; \infty \quad \text{(very high, multiplied by the loop gain)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **key advantage** of the non-inverting amplifier: it draws negligible current from the source. The KTU examiner frequently tests this distinction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier |
|---|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Closed-Loop Gain $A_v$ | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ |
| 2 | Phase Relationship | $180°$ inverted | $0°$ (in-phase) |
| 3 | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | $R_1$ | Very high ($\approx$ $\infty$) |
| 4 | Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ | Near zero | Near zero |
| 5 | Voltage at Inverting Pin $V_-$ | $0$ V (virtual ground) | $V_{in}$ |
| 6 | Bandwidth $f_{-3dB}$ | $\dfrac{GBW}{\vert A_v \vert}$ | $\dfrac{GBW}{A_v}$ |
| 7 | Maximum Output Swing | $\pm V_{sat} \approx \pm 13$ V (for 741) | $\pm V_{sat} \approx \pm 13$ V (for 741) |
| 8 | Condition for Linear Operation | $V_{out(pp)} \le 2 V_{sat}$ | $V_{out(pp)} \le 2 V_{sat}$ |

### 2.5 Bandwidth Limitation — The Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off

For a 741 op-amp, the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** is constant at **1 MHz**. This means as you increase the closed-loop gain, the usable bandwidth shrinks proportionally.

$$f_{-3dB} \;=\; \frac{GBW}{\vert A_v \vert} \;=\; \frac{1 \times 10^6 \, Hz}{\vert A_v \vert}$$

**Engineering Utility:** In practical applications such as audio pre-amplifiers, ECG/biomedical signal amplifiers, and sensor signal conditioning, this trade-off dictates component selection. High-gain DC amplifiers are inherently slow, and high-bandwidth amplifiers are inherently low-gain.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Trick:** If asked "What is the maximum gain you can design at 20 kHz audio bandwidth?" — the answer is $A_v(max) = 1 \times 10^6 / 20 \times 10^3 = 50$. Beyond this, the 741 will attenuate audio frequencies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Inverting Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain

**Given:** Ideal op-amp with infinite input impedance, infinite open-loop gain, and negative feedback through $R_f$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Virtual Short Concept.**
Since the non-inverting terminal is grounded,
$$V_+ \;=\; 0 \;V$$
By Rule 1 (Virtual Short), the inverting terminal follows the non-inverting terminal:
$$V_- \;=\; V_+ \;=\; 0 \;V$$
This is the "virtual ground" condition. Note that **no current actually flows to ground**; the terminal merely sits at 0 V potential.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node ($V_-$).**
The current entering the node from the input source through $R_1$ must equal the current leaving the node through the feedback resistor $R_f$ (since no current enters the op-amp's input terminal — Rule 2: Virtual Open).

Current through $R_1$ (from $V_{in}$ to $V_-$):
$$I_1 \;=\; \frac{V_{in} - V_-}{R_1} \;=\; \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} \;=\; \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

Current through $R_f$ (from $V_-$ to $V_{out}$):
$$I_f \;=\; \frac{V_- - V_{out}}{R_f} \;=\; \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} \;=\; -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL (Sum of currents entering the node = Sum of currents leaving).**
$$I_1 \;=\; I_f$$
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} \;=\; -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Gain.**
Rearranging:
$$V_{out} \;=\; -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$

Therefore, the closed-loop voltage gain is:
$$A_v \;=\; \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \;=\; -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

The negative sign explicitly confirms the **180° phase inversion** of the inverting amplifier topology. This completes the derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Non-Inverting Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain

**Given:** Ideal op-amp with negative feedback. Input $V_{in}$ applied to the non-inverting terminal. Resistor $R_1$ from inverting terminal to ground, resistor $R_f$ from inverting terminal to output.

**Step 1 — Apply the Virtual Short Concept.**
The non-inverting terminal is held at the input voltage:
$$V_+ \;=\; V_{in}$$
By Rule 1, the inverting terminal must equal the non-inverting terminal:
$$V_- \;=\; V_+ \;=\; V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the Voltage Divider at the Inverting Node.**
The resistors $R_1$ and $R_f$ form a voltage divider between $V_{out}$ and ground. The voltage at the junction ($V_-$) is:
$$V_- \;=\; V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions for $V_-$.**
$$V_{in} \;=\; V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Gain.**
Rearranging:
$$V_{out} \;=\; V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} \;=\; V_{in} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

Therefore, the closed-loop voltage gain is:
$$A_v \;=\; \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \;=\; 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

The result is **always positive and ≥ 1**, confirming the **in-phase, non-inverting** nature of this topology. The minimum gain is unity (1), which occurs when $R_f = 0$ (a special case called a **voltage follower** or **unity-gain buffer**). This completes the derivation.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** Design an inverting amplifier using a 741 op-amp with a closed-loop gain of $A_v = -10$. The input signal is a 100 mV peak, 1 kHz sine wave. Select standard resistor values and determine the theoretical output. Verify that the 741 op-amp's bandwidth is sufficient.

**Given Data:**
- $A_v = -10$
- $V_{in(peak)} = 100 \;mV = 0.1 \;V$
- $f_{in} = 1 \;kHz$
- $\mu$A741 parameters: GBW = 1 MHz, SR = 0.5 V/μs

**Step 1 — Choose $R_1$.**
A standard, low-noise choice is $R_1 = 10 \;k\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_f$.**
$$R_f \;=\; \vert A_v \vert \cdot R_1 \;=\; 10 \cdot 10 \;k\Omega \;=\; 100 \;k\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Theoretical Output.**
$$V_{out(peak)} \;=\; \vert A_v \vert \cdot V_{in(peak)} \;=\; 10 \cdot 0.1 \;V \;=\; 1.0 \;V$$
$$V_{out(pp)} \;=\; 2.0 \;V_{pp}$$

**Step 4 — Check Bandwidth Limit.**
$$f_{-3dB} \;=\; \frac{GBW}{\vert A_v \vert} \;=\; \frac{1 \times 10^6}{10} \;=\; 100 \;kHz$$

Since $f_{in} = 1$ kHz $\ll$ 100 kHz, the 741 will **not attenuate** the signal. **Design is feasible.** ✓

**Step 5 — Check Slew Rate Limit.**
The maximum rate of change for a sine wave of peak amplitude $V_p$ at frequency $f$ is:
$$SR_{required} \;=\; 2 \pi f V_p$$
$$SR_{required} \;=\; 2 \pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 1.0 \;=\; 6283 \;V/s \;=\; 0.00628 \;V/\mu s$$

Since 0.00628 V/μs $\ll$ 0.5 V/μs (741's SR), **no slew-rate distortion** will occur. ✓

**Final Design:** $R_1 = 10 \;k\Omega$, $R_f = 100 \;k\Omega$, theoretical $V_{out} = 1.0 \;V_{peak}$ at 1 kHz, with a 180° phase shift.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Selection Trade-offs

| Design Goal | Recommended Topology | Reason |
|-------------|----------------------|--------|
| High input impedance required | Non-Inverting | $Z_{in} \approx \infty$ does not load the source |
| Phase preservation critical | Non-Inverting | No 180° phase shift |
| Small input impedance is acceptable | Inverting | $Z_{in} = R_1$ can be made low to suppress noise |
| Need a virtual ground (summing junction) | Inverting | Virtual ground allows multiple inputs to be summed |
| Unity gain buffering | Non-Inverting (Voltage Follower) | $A_v = 1$ when $R_f = 0$ |
| Wide bandwidth at moderate gain | Either | Both share the same GBW constraint |
| Low component count, simple design | Inverting | Requires only 2 resistors |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question]** State the two fundamental assumptions (golden rules) used in the analysis of an ideal op-amp. Why are they valid for the 741 in the closed-loop configuration?

**Model Answer:**
The two assumptions are:
1. **Virtual Short:** The voltage difference between the inverting and non-inverting input terminals is zero, i.e., $V_+ = V_-$. This is valid because the open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$ and the finite differential input voltage $V_d$ produces $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_d$ which is bounded by the supply; thus $V_d \to 0$.
2. **Virtual Open:** No current flows into the op-amp input terminals, i.e., $I_{in} = 0$. This is valid because the input impedance of the 741 is approximately 2 MΩ (very large), so the input current is in the order of nanoamperes and can be neglected.

**(3 marks: 1.5 marks per rule + 1 mark for the justification)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question]** Compare the input impedance of an inverting and a non-inverting amplifier built using the same op-amp and the same resistor values. Which one is preferred in a sensor signal-conditioning application and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------------|
| Input Impedance | $Z_{in} = R_1$ (low, e.g., 10 kΩ) | $Z_{in} \approx \infty$ (very high, multiplied by loop gain) |

**Preferred topology:** **Non-Inverting Amplifier**.

**Reason:** In a sensor signal-conditioning application, the sensor (e.g., a thermocouple, photodiode, or bio-potential electrode) has a finite and often small source impedance. Loading this source with a low input impedance would attenuate the signal and introduce measurement errors. The non-inverting amplifier's high input impedance ensures that the sensor delivers its signal faithfully without loading effects.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for the comparison table + 2 marks for the application-level reasoning)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Inverting Amplifier Design Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** Design an inverting amplifier using a 741 op-amp to achieve a closed-loop voltage gain of $-20$. The input signal is a 200 mV peak, 2 kHz sine wave.

**(a)** Select suitable resistor values and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the theoretical output voltage, the -3 dB bandwidth, and verify the slew rate limitation. Draw the circuit diagram and tabulate the expected readings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $A_v = -20$, $V_{in(peak)} = 200 \;mV$, $f_{in} = 2$ kHz, μA741 op-amp.

**Step 1 — Choose $R_1$.**
A good engineering practice is to choose $R_1$ in the range of 1 kΩ to 100 kΩ. This minimizes the effect of input bias currents (which flow through $R_1$ and produce an offset voltage) while keeping input impedance reasonable. Selecting $R_1 = 10 \;k\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_f$.**
$$R_f \;=\; \vert A_v \vert \cdot R_1 \;=\; 20 \cdot 10 \;k\Omega \;=\; 200 \;k\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Check for Standard E12 Resistor Availability.**
$200 \;k\Omega$ is a standard E12 value. ✓

**Justification:** With $R_1 = 10 \;k\Omega$, the input impedance is 10 kΩ, which is acceptable for most signal generators (typically 50 Ω output impedance). The feedback resistor of 200 kΩ is well within the recommended range and avoids loading the output stage. The DC resistance seen by both inputs ($R_1 \parallel R_f$ at the inverting pin, and 0 Ω at the non-inverting pin) is approximately equal (≈ 9.5 kΩ vs 0 Ω), which slightly biases the offset — a small offset-null adjustment can be made via the 10 kΩ potentiometer between pins 1 and 5, with the wiper to $V_{EE}$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Theoretical Output Voltage.**
$$V_{out(peak)} \;=\; \vert A_v \vert \cdot V_{in(peak)} \;=\; 20 \cdot 0.2 \;V \;=\; 4.0 \;V$$
$$V_{out(pp)} \;=\; 8.0 \;V_{pp}$$

This is within the 741's linear output swing of approximately ±13 V. ✓

**Step 2 — Theoretical -3 dB Bandwidth.**
$$f_{-3dB} \;=\; \frac{GBW}{\vert A_v \vert} \;=\; \frac{1 \times 10^6 \;Hz}{20} \;=\; 50 \;kHz$$

Since $f_{in} = 2 \;kHz \ll 50 \;kHz$, the amplifier operates well within its linear bandwidth. ✓

**Step 3 — Slew Rate Check.**
The required slew rate for a 2 kHz sine wave with 4.0 V peak amplitude:
$$SR_{required} \;=\; 2 \pi f V_p \;=\; 2 \pi \cdot 2000 \cdot 4.0 \;=\; 50265 \;V/s \;\approx\; 0.0503 \;V/\mu s$$

The 741's maximum SR is 0.5 V/μs, and $0.0503 \ll 0.5$. No slew-rate distortion. ✓

**Circuit Diagram (Description for the Record):**
```
        R1 = 10kΩ
Vin o──/\/\/\──┬── (Pin 2: Inverting Input)
              │
              │  Rf = 200kΩ
              └──/\/\/\──┬── (Pin 6: Output) ──o Vout
                          │
            ┌─────────────┘
            │ (Pin 6 connects back to Rf)
        (Pin 3: Non-Inverting) ──o GND
        (Pin 7: V+ = +15V)
        (Pin 4: V- = -15V)
```

**Expected Observation Table:**

| $V_{in(peak)}$ (mV) | $V_{out(peak)}$ Theoretical (V) | $V_{out(peak)}$ Practical (V) | Phase Shift (degrees) | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | 1.0 | ~0.97 | 180 | ~3% |
| 100 | 2.0 | ~1.94 | 180 | ~3% |
| 150 | 3.0 | ~2.91 | 180 | ~3% |
| 200 | 4.0 | ~3.88 | 180 | ~3% |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Non-Inverting Amplifier Design Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model]** Design a non-inverting amplifier using a 741 op-amp with a voltage gain of 11. The available input is 50 mV peak at 5 kHz.

**(a)** Determine the resistor values, draw the circuit, and explain why the non-inverting topology is preferred for buffering applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the expected output, the bandwidth, and discuss what happens to the output if the input frequency is increased to 80 kHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $A_v = 11$, $V_{in(peak)} = 50 \;mV$, $f_{in} = 5$ kHz.

**Step 1 — Choose $R_1$ and Calculate $R_f$.**
The non-inverting gain formula is:
$$A_v \;=\; 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

Setting $A_v = 11$:
$$11 \;=\; 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{R_f}{R_1} \;=\; 10$$

Choose $R_1 = 10 \;k\Omega$, then $R_f = 100 \;k\Omega$. Both are standard E12 values. ✓

**Step 2 — Draw the Circuit (Description):**
```
              R1 = 10kΩ
              ┌──/\/\/\──┐
              │           │
Vin o─────────┴─(Pin 3: Non-Inverting)    (Pin 6: Output) ──o Vout
                                            │
              ┌──/\/\/\──┐                  │
              │           │                  │
              │   Rf = 100kΩ ────────────────┘
              │
              (Pin 2: Inverting)
              
        (Pin 7: V+ = +15V)
        (Pin 4: V- = -15V)
```

**Step 3 — Why Non-Inverting for Buffering:**
A **voltage follower** (a special case where $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$, giving $A_v = 1$) is the most common buffering configuration. It has:
- Unity gain (output exactly equals input)
- Extremely high input impedance (≈ ∞)
- Extremely low output impedance (≈ 0)

This allows it to act as an **impedance transformer**: a high-impedance source (e.g., a sensor) can drive a low-impedance load (e.g., a long cable or speaker) without attenuation. The inverting topology cannot achieve this because its input impedance is limited to $R_1$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Expected Output Voltage.**
$$V_{out(peak)} \;=\; A_v \cdot V_{in(peak)} \;=\; 11 \cdot 0.05 \;V \;=\; 0.55 \;V$$
$$V_{out(pp)} \;=\; 1.10 \;V_{pp}$$

**Step 2 — Expected -3 dB Bandwidth.**
$$f_{-3dB} \;=\; \frac{GBW}{A_v} \;=\; \frac{1 \times 10^6 \;Hz}{11} \;\approx\; 90.9 \;kHz$$

**Step 3 — Behavior at 80 kHz.**
At 80 kHz, the frequency is approaching the -3 dB cutoff (90.9 kHz). The gain will be:
$$A_v(f) \;\approx\; \frac{A_v}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{-3dB})^2}}$$
$$A_v(80 \,kHz) \;\approx\; \frac{11}{\sqrt{1 + (80/90.9)^2}} \;\approx\; \frac{11}{\sqrt{1.774}} \;\approx\; 8.26$$

So at 80 kHz, the practical gain drops from 11 to about **8.26**, and a **phase lag** of about $\arctan(80/90.9) \approx 41.4°$ is introduced. The output peak voltage would be:
$$V_{out(peak)} \;\approx\; 8.26 \cdot 0.05 \;V \;=\; 0.413 \;V$$

**Conclusion:** The 741 op-amp is unsuitable for high-gain (> 10) amplification at frequencies above ~50 kHz. For such applications, a **wideband op-amp** such as the LF351 or TL081 (GBW = 3 MHz) or the LM318 (GBW = 15 MHz) must be used.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (3-Mark Loss Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the inverting amplifier gain formula. Always write $A_v = -R_f/R_1$, not $A_v = R_f/R_1$. The negative sign is non-negotiable.
> 2. **Confusing input impedance** between the two topologies. Inverting: $Z_{in} = R_1$ (low). Non-inverting: $Z_{in} \approx \infty$ (high). This is the most-asked viva question.
> 3. **Forgetting to check the GBW constraint.** A design that violates $f_{in} \cdot \vert A_v \vert \le GBW$ is technically incorrect, even if the resistor values are right.
> 4. **Drawing the wrong op-amp pin configuration.** Pin 2 = Inverting (-), Pin 3 = Non-Inverting (+), Pin 6 = Output, Pin 7 = $V_{CC}$, Pin 4 = $V_{EE}$. Drawing these backwards will cost 2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning the virtual ground concept** in the inverting amplifier — it is the *conceptual heart* of the derivation and the examiner expects it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Lab Record / Viva:**

- The **741 op-amp** has 8 pins. Memorize: **2 = (-)**, **3 = (+)**, **6 = OUT**, **7 = $V^+$**, **4 = $V^-$**. Pins 1 and 5 are for offset null (optional).
- **Inverting Amplifier Gain:** $A_v = -R_f/R_1$. The output is **180° out of phase** with the input.
- **Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain:** $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_1$. The output is **in phase** with the input.
- **Virtual Ground:** Exists **only** in the inverting configuration. The inverting pin sits at 0 V even though it is not physically connected to ground.
- **Virtual Short:** Always holds in any negative-feedback configuration: $V_+ = V_-$.
- **Virtual Open:** $I_{in} \approx 0$ at the op-amp terminals. All current flows through the external resistors.
- **Input Impedance:** Inverting → low ($= R_1$); Non-inverting → very high ($\approx \infty$).
- **GBW Product:** Constant at 1 MHz for 741. Higher gain ⇒ lower bandwidth.
- **Slew Rate:** 0.5 V/μs for 741. For a sine wave of peak $V_p$ at frequency $f$, $SR_{req} = 2\pi f V_p$.
- **Saturation Voltage:** $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13$ V (for ±15 V supply). Output cannot exceed this in linear operation.
- **Standard Resistor Choice:** Use E12 series values (1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.2, 2.7, 3.3, 3.9, 4.7, 5.6, 6.8, 8.2 × 10ⁿ). $R_1$ between 1 kΩ and 100 kΩ.
- **Practical Setup Order:** Connect power supply first, check $V^+$ and $V^-$, then wire the input network, then the feedback network, then apply the signal.
- **Measurement Procedure:** Use **CRO in DC coupling mode**, measure $V_{in(pp)}$ on CH1 and $V_{out(pp)}$ on CH2, calculate practical gain = $V_{out(pp)} / V_{in(pp)}$.
- **Phase Verification:** Use **CRO XY mode** (Lissajous figure) or measure time delay between zero crossings of input and output to confirm 180° shift (inverting) or 0° shift (non-inverting).
- **Percentage Error Formula:**
$$\% \text{ Error} \;=\; \left\vert \frac{A_{practical} - A_{theoretical}}{A_{theoretical}} \right\vert \times 100$$
- An error of **2-5%** is acceptable in the KTU lab. Errors > 10% indicate a wiring fault or faulty component.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Integrator and Differentiator Circuits Using Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL308 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RC Integrator and Differentiator Circuits Using Op-Amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Op-Amp based RC Integrator** is an active linear circuit that produces an output voltage waveform which is the time integral of the input voltage waveform, scaled by a factor of $-1/RC$. It is constructed using an operational amplifier in the inverting configuration with a resistor $R$ at the input and a capacitor $C$ in the feedback path. The output relationship is governed by the equation:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_{out}(0)$$

Conversely, an **Op-Amp based RC Differentiator** is an active linear circuit whose output voltage is proportional to the time derivative of the input voltage, scaled by a factor of $-RC$. The capacitor $C$ is placed at the input and the resistor $R$ is used in the feedback loop. The governing equation is:

$$V_{out}(t) = -RC \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The negative sign in both equations is due to the inverting configuration of the op-amp. A non-inverting output can be obtained by cascading an inverting amplifier with unity gain, or by swapping the input terminals. The "RC" in both circuits is a fundamental **time constant** ($\tau = RC$) and dictates the frequency response behaviour of the circuit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are driving a car and looking at the **speedometer** (this shows the *derivative* of distance, i.e., speed). Now, suppose the speedometer needle is fed into an integrator circuit — the output would represent the **total distance travelled**. This is essentially what an integrator does: it accumulates the input over time.

**Real-world analogies:**

- **Integrator** → A *water tank filling process*. If the input is the rate of water flow (in litres/second), the integrator's output is the total volume of water in the tank (litres). The capacitor acts as the storage tank, and the resistor controls the flow rate.
- **Differentiator** → A *speedometer in a car*. The input is the position (distance), and the output is the rate of change of position (velocity). The capacitor senses *change* — it resists sudden changes in voltage, while the resistor converts the resulting current into a measurable voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use an Op-Amp?** A passive RC integrator (just a resistor and capacitor) has severe loading problems — the output voltage is heavily affected by whatever is connected to it. The op-amp provides **infinite input impedance** (no current drawn from the source) and **zero output impedance** (the load doesn't affect the output). This **buffering** action makes the op-amp based integrator and differentiator ideal for laboratory and instrumentation applications.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Resistance | $R$ | **Ohms ($\Omega$)** | $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $100\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| Capacitance | $C$ | **Farads (F)** | $0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ to $1\,\mu\text{F}$ |
| Time constant | $\tau = RC$ | **Seconds (s)** | $10\,\mu\text{s}$ to $100\,\text{ms}$ |
| Op-amp supply | $V_{CC}, -V_{EE}$ | **Volts (V)** | $\pm 12\,\text{V}$ to $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ |
| Cut-off frequency | $f_c$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | $1\,\text{kHz}$ to $100\,\text{kHz}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 The Op-Amp Integrator (Miller Integrator)

**Circuit topology:** Resistor $R$ connects the input signal to the inverting terminal; capacitor $C$ is the feedback element from output to the inverting terminal. The non-inverting terminal is grounded.

**Step-by-step logic of operation:**

- **Step 1 — Virtual Ground Concept:** Due to negative feedback and the high open-loop gain ($A_{OL} \to \infty$), the inverting terminal is held at the same potential as the non-inverting terminal (which is at $0\,\text{V}$). Hence, the inverting node is a **virtual ground** ($V^- = 0\,\text{V}$).

- **Step 2 — Current Through Resistor:** The input current through $R$ is $I_{in} = (V_{in} - 0)/R = V_{in}/R$. Since the op-amp input impedance is infinite, **no current flows into the op-amp terminals**.

- **Step 3 — Current Through Capacitor:** By Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL), the same current must flow through the feedback capacitor: $I_C = I_{in} = V_{in}/R$.

- **Step 4 — Capacitor I-V Relationship:** The current through a capacitor is related to the voltage across it by $I_C = C \cdot dV/dt$. The voltage across $C$ is $(V^- - V_{out}) = (0 - V_{out}) = -V_{out}$. Hence, $I_C = C \cdot d(0 - V_{out})/dt = -C \cdot dV_{out}/dt$.

- **Step 5 — Equate and Integrate:** Setting the two expressions for $I_C$ equal: $-C \cdot dV_{out}/dt = V_{in}/R$. Rearranging and integrating gives the integrator equation $V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{in}\,dt$.

#### 2.1.2 The Op-Amp Differentiator

**Circuit topology:** Capacitor $C$ connects the input signal to the inverting terminal; resistor $R$ is the feedback element. The non-inverting terminal is grounded.

**Step-by-step logic of operation:**

- **Step 1 — Virtual Ground:** As before, $V^- = 0\,\text{V}$ (virtual ground).

- **Step 2 — Current Through Capacitor:** The input current is $I_{in} = C \cdot d(V_{in} - 0)/dt = C \cdot dV_{in}/dt$.

- **Step 3 — KCL at Inverting Node:** No current enters the op-amp, so the same current flows through the feedback resistor: $I_R = I_{in}$.

- **Step 4 — Resistor Voltage:** The voltage across $R$ is $V_R = (V^- - V_{out}) = -V_{out} = I_R \cdot R$.

- **Step 5 — Equate:** $-V_{out} = R \cdot C \cdot dV_{in}/dt$, yielding $V_{out} = -RC \cdot dV_{in}/dt$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Board Exams:** Memorize the table below. Nearly every numerical or derivation problem on Op-Amp integrators/differentiators uses one of these formulas.

| Circuit Type | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | Feedback Impedance $Z_f$ | Transfer Function $H(j\omega)$ | Time-Domain Equation | Cut-off Frequency $f_c$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Integrator** | $R$ | $1/(j\omega C)$ | $H(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{j\omega RC}$ | $V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt$ | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Differentiator** | $1/(j\omega C)$ | $R$ | $H(j\omega) = -j\omega RC$ | $V_{out} = -RC\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Practical Differentiator** | $R_1 \vert\vert (1/j\omega C_1)$ | $R_f$ | $H(j\omega) = -\frac{R_f \cdot j\omega C_1}{(1+j\omega R_1 C_1)}$ | Approximate differentiator | $f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$, $f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_1}$ |
| **Practical Integrator** | $R_1$ | $R_f \vert\vert (1/j\omega C_F)$ | $H(j\omega) = -\frac{1+j\omega R_f C_F}{j\omega R_1 C_F}$ | Approximate integrator | $f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_F}$ |

**Key Boundary Conditions for Ideal Operation:**

- Integrator is ideal for $\omega \gg \omega_c$ (i.e., input frequency much greater than $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$).
- Differentiator is ideal for $\omega \ll \omega_c$ (i.e., input frequency much less than $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Integrator Applications:** Ramp generators, A/D converters (dual-slope), analog computers (solving differential equations), waveform shapers (converting square wave to triangular wave), and PID controllers (the I-term).
- **Differentiator Applications:** Edge detectors in digital signal processing, PID controllers (the D-term), FM demodulators, and lead-lag compensators in control systems.
- **Laboratory Utility:** The op-amp integrator is a fundamental building block for active filters and is widely used in biomedical instrumentation (e.g., ECG signal processing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Pitfall:** A pure (ideal) differentiator is highly susceptible to high-frequency noise. In the laboratory, students use a **practical differentiator** which adds a small resistor $R_1$ in series with $C$ and a small capacitor $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$ to limit the high-frequency gain and prevent oscillations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ideal Integrator Output for a Square Wave Input

**Problem Statement:** An op-amp integrator uses $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. A square wave of amplitude $\pm 5\,\text{V}$ and frequency $1\,\text{kHz}$ is applied. Derive the expression for the output voltage and determine the peak-to-peak output voltage.

**Step 1: Identify the time constant.**

The time constant of the integrator is given by:

$$\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3) \times (0.1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2: Identify the input waveform parameters.**

A square wave of frequency $f = 1\,\text{kHz}$ has a period:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

Thus, the half-period (duration of one constant level) is $T/2 = 0.5\,\text{ms}$.

**Step 3: Apply the integrator equation during the positive half-cycle.**

During $0 \leq t < T/2$, the input is $V_{in} = +5\,\text{V}$. Using the integrator equation:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_0^{t} V_{in}\,dt + V_{out}(0)$$

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{10^{-3}} \int_0^{t} 5\,dt + V_{out}(0)$$

$$V_{out}(t) = -5000 t + V_{out}(0)$$

This is a **linearly decreasing ramp** with slope $-5000\,\text{V/s}$.

**Step 4: Compute the change in output over the half-period.**

At $t = T/2 = 0.5\,\text{ms} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}$:

$$\Delta V_{out} = -5000 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = -2.5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5: Apply the integrator equation during the negative half-cycle.**

During $T/2 \leq t < T$, the input is $V_{in} = -5\,\text{V}$. The output is a linearly increasing ramp with slope $+5000\,\text{V/s}$. The change in output over this half-period is also $+2.5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 6: Compute peak-to-peak output voltage.**

Since the output is a triangular wave symmetric about its mean, the peak-to-peak output is:

$$V_{out(pp)} = 2 \times 2.5 = 5\,\text{V}_{pp}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **General Formula for Square Wave Input:** For a square wave of amplitude $\pm V_m$ and frequency $f$ applied to an ideal integrator, the output is a triangular wave with peak-to-peak amplitude:
> $$V_{out(pp)} = \frac{V_m}{2fRC}$$
> Substituting: $V_{out(pp)} = 5 / (2 \times 1000 \times 10^{-3}) = 5/2 = 2.5\,\text{V}_{pp}$... wait, let me recompute. Actually, $V_m = 5\,\text{V}$, and the change over half-period is $V_m \cdot T/(2RC) = 5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}/10^{-3} = 2.5\,\text{V}$. So peak-to-peak is $5\,\text{V}_{pp}$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ideal Differentiator Output for a Triangular Wave Input

**Problem Statement:** An op-amp differentiator uses $R = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$. A triangular wave of peak amplitude $\pm 4\,\text{V}$ and period $T = 2\,\text{ms}$ is applied. Find the output waveform.

**Step 1: Identify the time constant.**

$$\tau = RC = (100 \times 10^3) \times (0.01 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2: Compute the slope of the input triangular wave.**

The triangular wave rises from $-4\,\text{V}$ to $+4\,\text{V}$ over a time of $T/2 = 1\,\text{ms}$. The slope is:

$$m = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t} = \frac{4 - (-4)}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{8}{10^{-3}} = 8000\,\text{V/s}$$

**Step 3: Apply the differentiator equation for the rising portion.**

During the rising slope, $dV_{in}/dt = +8000\,\text{V/s}$. Therefore:

$$V_{out} = -RC \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -(10^{-3}) \times 8000 = -8\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4: Apply the differentiator equation for the falling portion.**

During the falling slope, $dV_{in}/dt = -8000\,\text{V/s}$. Therefore:

$$V_{out} = -RC \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -(10^{-3}) \times (-8000) = +8\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5: Write the final output expression.**

The output is a **square wave** of amplitude $\pm 8\,\text{V}$ and same period $T = 2\,\text{ms}$ as the input. The output is **inverted** (negative during the rising slope, positive during the falling slope).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the dual of the previous result. An integrator converts a square wave to a triangular wave, and a differentiator converts a triangular wave to a square wave. This is the basis of **function generator circuits** used extensively in laboratories.

### 3.3 Frequency Response Analysis (Bode Plot Insight)

**Integrator:** The magnitude of the transfer function is $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = 1/(\omega RC)$, and the phase is $-90°$ (constant). On a Bode plot, this is a straight line with slope $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$, crossing $0\,\text{dB}$ at $\omega = 1/(RC)$.

**Differentiator:** The magnitude is $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \omega RC$, and the phase is $+90°$ (constant). On a Bode plot, this is a straight line with slope $+20\,\text{dB/decade}$, crossing $0\,\text{dB}$ at $\omega = 1/(RC)$.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Ideal Integrator | Ideal Differentiator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Input element | Resistor $R$ | Capacitor $C$ |
| Feedback element | Capacitor $C$ | Resistor $R$ |
| DC gain | Infinite (unstable) | Zero |
| High-frequency gain | Zero | Infinite (unstable) |
| Phase shift | $-90°$ (lag) | $+90°$ (lead) |
| Stability | Marginal — needs $R_f$ in practice | Poor — needs $R_{in}$ in practice |
| Useful frequency range | $\omega \gg 1/RC$ | $\omega \ll 1/RC$ |
| Square wave input → | Triangular wave output | Impulse spikes at edges |
| Triangular wave input → | Parabolic wave output | Square wave output |
| Sine wave input → | Cosine wave output (lagging) | Cosine wave output (leading) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Op-Amp Integrator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Vin Square Wave] -- R 10k --> nodeB[Inverting Node Virtual Ground]
    nodeC[Ground 0V] --> nodeD[Non Inverting Input]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Op Amp uA741]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -- Feedback --> nodeF[Capacitor C 0.1uF]
    nodeF -- to inverting input --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeG[Vout Triangular Wave]
```

**Architecture Flow Description:**

The input square wave $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting terminal of the op-amp through a resistor $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. The non-inverting terminal is connected to ground. A capacitor $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ provides negative feedback from the output back to the inverting input. The op-amp (typically $\mu$A741) forces the inverting node to a virtual ground, ensuring that the input current $I_{in} = V_{in}/R$ flows entirely through the capacitor. The output $V_{out}$ is the time integral of the input, producing a triangular waveform from a square wave input.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Op-Amp Differentiator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Vin Triangular Wave] -- C 0.01uF --> nodeB[Inverting Node Virtual Ground]
    nodeC[Ground 0V] --> nodeD[Non Inverting Input]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Op Amp uA741]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -- Feedback --> nodeF[Resistor R 100k]
    nodeF -- to inverting input --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeG[Vout Square Wave]
```

**Architecture Flow Description:**

The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting terminal through a capacitor $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$. The non-inverting terminal is grounded. A resistor $R = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ in the feedback path converts the capacitor current $I_C = C \cdot dV_{in}/dt$ into an output voltage $V_{out} = -RC \cdot dV_{in}/dt$. The op-amp ensures virtual ground at the inverting node, isolating the differentiating action from any loading effects.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Practical Differentiator (with Stability Components)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Vin] -- R1 small --> nodeJ[Junction]
    nodeJ -- C1 main --> nodeB[Inverting Node]
    nodeC[Ground 0V] --> nodeD[Non Inverting Input]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Op Amp]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -- Rf feedback --> nodeK[Output Junction]
    nodeK -- Cf small --> nodeB
    nodeK --> nodeG[Vout]
```

**Architecture Flow Description:**

The practical differentiator adds a small series resistor $R_1$ at the input to limit high-frequency gain and a small feedback capacitor $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$ to introduce a pole that rolls off the gain at high frequencies. The component values are chosen such that $R_1 C_1 = R_f C_f$, ensuring that the high-frequency noise is attenuated. The two cut-off frequencies are $f_a = 1/(2\pi R_1 C_1)$ and $f_b = 1/(2\pi R_f C_1)$, and the useful differentiating range lies between $f_a$ and $f_b$.

### 4.4 Signal Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Square Wave Vin] --> procA[Op Amp Integrator]
    procA --> outA[Triangular Wave Vout]

    inputB[Triangular Wave Vin] --> procB[Op Amp Differentiator]
    procB --> outB[Square Wave Vout]

    inputC[Sine Wave Vin] --> procC[Op Amp Integrator]
    procC --> outC[Cosine Wave Vout lagging 90 deg]

    inputD[Sine Wave Vin] --> procD[Op Amp Differentiator]
    procD --> outD[Cosine Wave Vout leading 90 deg]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Mermaid Diagrams:** Since the op-amp integrator and differentiator are simple single-stage circuits with a few passive components, the Mermaid diagrams represent the **functional signal flow** from input through the op-amp to output. The arrows depict the path of signal propagation and the feedback action. The actual physical circuit schematic includes standard connections: $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ DC supply to pins 7 and 4 of the $\mu$A741, offset null adjustment at pins 1 and 5, and output at pin 6.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define an op-amp based integrator. Derive its output voltage expression.**

**Model Answer:**

An op-amp based integrator is an active circuit using an operational amplifier with a resistor $R$ at the input and a capacitor $C$ in the feedback path. The output voltage is proportional to the integral of the input voltage.

**Derivation:** Since the inverting terminal is at virtual ground ($V^- = 0$), the input current is $I_{in} = V_{in}/R$. This current flows through the feedback capacitor, so $I_C = C \cdot d(0 - V_{out})/dt = -C \cdot dV_{out}/dt$. Equating: $-C \cdot dV_{out}/dt = V_{in}/R$. Integrating both sides:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{in}\,dt + V_{out}(0)$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] List any three applications of op-amp differentiator circuits.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **FM Demodulators** — The differentiator converts frequency variations in an FM signal into corresponding amplitude variations, which can then be detected by an envelope detector.
2. **PID Controllers** — The derivative (D) action in industrial PID controllers is implemented using op-amp differentiator circuits to provide lead compensation.
3. **Waveform Generators** — A triangular-to-square wave conversion is achieved using an op-amp differentiator, useful in function generator circuits.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an op-amp integrator. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an op-amp integrator with $R = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.05\,\mu\text{F}$, determine the output voltage when a square wave of amplitude $\pm 6\,\text{V}$ and frequency $2\,\text{kHz}$ is applied. Assume zero initial condition. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Circuit Diagram and Operation:**

The op-amp integrator consists of:
- Resistor $R$ connected between input $V_{in}$ and the inverting terminal (pin 2 of $\mu$A741).
- Capacitor $C$ connected between the output (pin 6) and the inverting terminal (feedback).
- Non-inverting terminal (pin 3) connected to ground.
- DC supply $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ at pins 7 and 4.

**Operation:** When a positive input is applied, current $I_{in} = V_{in}/R$ flows through $R$. Since the op-amp draws no input current, this entire current flows into the capacitor. The capacitor charges linearly, causing the output to ramp down (negative) at a rate of $V_{in}/(RC)$. For a negative input, the output ramps up linearly.

**Derivation:**

Applying KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground at 0 V):

$$I_{in} = I_C$$

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = C \cdot \frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt}$$

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

$$dV_{out} = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC}\,dt$$

Integrating both sides:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_0^{t} V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_{out}(0)$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $R = 20\,\text{k}\Omega = 20 \times 10^3\,\Omega$, $C = 0.05\,\mu\text{F} = 0.05 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{F}$, square wave amplitude $V_m = 6\,\text{V}$, frequency $f = 2\,\text{kHz}$, $V_{out}(0) = 0$.

**Step 1: Compute the time constant.**

$$\tau = RC = (20 \times 10^3)(0.05 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2: Compute the half-period of the square wave.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{2000} = 0.5\,\text{ms}$$

$$\frac{T}{2} = 0.25\,\text{ms} = 0.25 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the change in output during the positive half-cycle.**

During the positive half-cycle ($V_{in} = +6\,\text{V}$, $0 \leq t < T/2$):

$$V_{out}(T/2) = -\frac{1}{RC} \cdot V_m \cdot \frac{T}{2} = -\frac{6 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}} = -1.5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4: Compute the change in output during the negative half-cycle.**

During the negative half-cycle ($V_{in} = -6\,\text{V}$, $T/2 \leq t < T$):

$$\Delta V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} \cdot (-6) \cdot \frac{T}{2} = +1.5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5: Final output waveform.**

The output is a **triangular wave** of peak-to-peak amplitude:

$$V_{out(pp)} = 1.5 + 1.5 = 3\,\text{V}_{pp}$$

with mean value $0\,\text{V}$ and frequency $2\,\text{kHz}$ (same as input).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of an op-amp differentiator with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a practical differentiator circuit to differentiate an input signal whose frequency ranges from $100\,\text{Hz}$ to $1\,\text{kHz}$. Assume $C_1 = 1\,\mu\text{F}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Circuit Diagram and Operation:**

The op-amp differentiator consists of:
- Capacitor $C$ connected between input $V_{in}$ and the inverting terminal.
- Resistor $R$ connected between the output and the inverting terminal (feedback).
- Non-inverting terminal grounded.

**Operation:** The capacitor differentiates the input voltage (i.e., passes only the rate of change). The current through $C$ is $I_C = C \cdot dV_{in}/dt$. This current flows through the feedback resistor, producing an output voltage proportional to the derivative of the input.

**Derivation:**

At the inverting node (virtual ground, $V^- = 0$):

$$I_{in} = I_R$$

$$C \cdot \frac{d(V_{in} - 0)}{dt} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R}$$

$$C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R}$$

$$V_{out}(t) = -RC \cdot \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

**(b) Practical Differentiator Design:**

**Given:** Input frequency range $f_{in} = 100\,\text{Hz}$ to $1\,\text{kHz}$, $C_1 = 1\,\mu\text{F}$.

**Step 1: Choose the useful differentiating frequency range.**

For a practical differentiator, the input frequency should lie between the two cut-off frequencies $f_a$ and $f_b$, where $f_a < f_b$. We choose:

- Lower cut-off $f_a = 100\,\text{Hz}$ (so the differentiator is accurate above this).
- Upper cut-off $f_b = 10 \times f_{max} = 10 \times 1\,\text{kHz} = 10\,\text{kHz}$ (to ensure noise attenuation at higher frequencies).

**Step 2: Compute $R_1$ from $f_a$.**

$$f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$$

$$R_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_a C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 100 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{6.283 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.5\,\Omega \approx 1.6\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Compute $R_f$ and $C_f$ from $f_b$.**

The condition for a practical differentiator is $R_1 C_1 = R_f C_f$. Choose $C_f = 0.01\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\,\text{F}$ (a small feedback capacitor to limit high-frequency gain).

$$R_f = \frac{1}{2\pi f_b C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10000 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{0.06283} \approx 15.9\,\Omega$$

$$C_f = \frac{R_1 C_1}{R_f} = \frac{1591.5 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{15.9} \approx 1 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{F} = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$$

**Final Design Values:**

- $R_1 \approx 1.6\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $C_1 = 1\,\mu\text{F}$
- $R_f \approx 15.9\,\Omega$
- $C_f = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$

The differentiator will accurately differentiate signals in the range $100\,\text{Hz}$ to $1\,\text{kHz}$, with the noise above $10\,\text{kHz}$ attenuated at a rate of $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the output equation — Examiners specifically check for the inverting configuration. Losing the sign costs 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing $RC$ with $1/(RC)$** — In the integrator, the output is proportional to $1/(RC) \int V_{in}\,dt$, NOT to $RC \int V_{in}\,dt$. Many students swap these and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating the virtual ground assumption** — This is the foundation of the derivation. Without explicitly stating $V^- = V^+ = 0\,\text{V}$, the derivation is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting initial conditions** — In integrator problems, the constant of integration $V_{out}(0)$ must be mentioned. If the problem states "zero initial condition," then $V_{out}(0) = 0$ must be explicitly written.
> 5. **Mixing up the practical differentiator formulas** — Students often confuse $R_1 C_1$ with $R_f C_f$ in the design equations. Remember: the design condition is $R_1 C_1 = R_f C_f$, and the differentiating range lies between $f_a = 1/(2\pi R_1 C_1)$ and $f_b = 1/(2\pi R_f C_1)$.
> 6. **Units inconsistency** — Always convert $k\Omega$ to $\Omega$ and $\mu\text{F}$ to $\text{F}$ before multiplication. Failure to do so gives answers that are off by a factor of $10^9$ and cost 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Integrator equation:** $V_{out} = -(1/RC) \int V_{in}\,dt$ — output is the **negative integral** of the input.
- **Differentiator equation:** $V_{out} = -RC \cdot dV_{in}/dt$ — output is the **negative derivative** of the input.
- **Virtual ground concept** is the foundation of every op-amp circuit analysis with negative feedback.
- **Square wave in → Triangular wave out** for an integrator (and vice-versa for a differentiator).
- **The ideal integrator has infinite DC gain**, which causes drift and saturation. A practical integrator adds a feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$ to limit the DC gain to $-R_f/R$.
- **The ideal differentiator has infinite high-frequency gain**, which amplifies noise. A practical differentiator adds an input resistor $R_1$ and a feedback capacitor $C_f$ to limit the high-frequency gain and ensure stability.
- **Cut-off frequency** for both circuits is $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$.
- **Phase shift:** Integrator lags by $90°$; Differentiator leads by $90°$.
- **Useful frequency range:** Integrator works well for $\omega \gg 1/RC$; Differentiator works well for $\omega \ll 1/RC$.
- **Sine wave input:** Integrator output is a cosine wave (lagging); Differentiator output is a cosine wave (leading).
- **The $RC$ time constant** is the most important parameter — it determines the slope of the output ramp and the amplitude of the output triangular wave.
- **Practical integrators** use $R_f$ in the feedback to prevent saturation due to input offset voltage and bias current.
- **Practical differentiators** use $R_1$ in series with $C$ and $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$ to prevent high-frequency oscillation.
- **Key relationship:** $R_1 C_1 = R_f C_f$ for a properly designed practical differentiator.
- **$\mu$A741 pin configuration to remember:** Pin 2 = Inverting input, Pin 3 = Non-inverting input, Pin 6 = Output, Pin 7 = $+V_{CC}$, Pin 4 = $-V_{EE}$, Pins 1 and 5 = Offset null.
- **KTU 2024 scheme emphasis:** Expect a 7-mark derivation problem on either integrator or differentiator, and a 7-mark design or numerical problem. Practice drawing the circuit diagram neatly — it carries **2 marks** in every KTU ESE question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Set Up of Active Low Pass and High Pass Filters Using Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL308 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Set Up of Active Low Pass and High Pass Filters Using Op-Amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Electronic Filter?

A **filter** in electronics is a frequency-selective two-port network designed to pass signals within a specified range of frequencies (the **passband**) while attenuating signals outside this range (the **stopband**). The boundary frequency that separates the passband from the stopband is called the **cutoff frequency ($f_c$)** or **3-dB frequency ($f_{3dB}$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A filter is a circuit that transmits (passes) signals of desired frequencies and rejects (attenuates) signals of undesired frequencies. The frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to **70.7%** ($\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$) of its maximum passband value is defined as the cutoff frequency, corresponding to a **−3 dB** gain point.

## 1.2 Classification of Filters Based on Frequency Response

| Filter Type | Passband | Stopband | Function |
|:-----------:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|
| **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** | $0$ to $f_c$ | $f > f_c$ | Passes low frequencies, blocks high |
| **High Pass Filter (HPF)** | $f > f_c$ | $0$ to $f_c$ | Passes high frequencies, blocks low |
| **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** | $f_1$ to $f_2$ | Outside band | Passes a band, blocks rest |
| **Band Stop Filter (BSF)** | Outside $f_1$–$f_2$ | Within band | Blocks a band, passes rest |

## 1.3 Active vs. Passive Filters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Active Filters?** Unlike passive filters (built using only R, L, C), active filters use an active component like an **op-amp (Operational Amplifier)** along with passive elements. The op-amp provides **voltage gain, buffering, and high input impedance**, eliminating the loading effect and eliminating the need for bulky inductors.

| Property | Passive Filter | Active Filter |
|:--------:|:-------------:|:-------------:|
| Active Device | None | Op-amp / Transistor |
| Gain | Unity or less ($\leq 1$) | Greater than unity (Adjustable) |
| Loading Effect | Severe | Negligible (High $Z_{in}$) |
| Inductors Required | Yes (for certain designs) | No |
| Power Supply | Not required | Required (±V) |
| Frequency Range | Wide (limited by L) | Limited by op-amp GBW |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Sieve" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Intuition:** Imagine a **water sieve** that lets only small particles (low frequencies) pass through and blocks large stones (high frequencies) — that is a **Low Pass Filter**. Conversely, a sieve that lets only large stones (high frequencies) pass and blocks sand (low frequencies) is a **High Pass Filter**.
>
> In audio engineering, a **Low Pass Filter** removes high-frequency hiss/noise (treble cut), while a **High Pass Filter** removes low-frequency rumble (bass cut). In a subwoofer system, the LPF ensures only bass reaches the subwoofer, and the HPF (tweeter crossover) sends only treble to the small speakers.

## 1.5 Why Op-Amp 741 in this Lab?

The **IC 741** (µA741) is the standard general-purpose op-amp used in KTU undergraduate labs. Key specifications:
- **Supply Voltage:** $\pm 12$ V to $\pm 18$ V
- **Open-Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$):** $\approx 2 \times 10^5$ (typical)
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):** **1 MHz**
- **Slew Rate:** **0.5 V/µs**
- **Input Impedance:** $\approx 2$ MΩ
- **Output Impedance:** $\approx 75$ Ω

These parameters govern the **useful operating frequency range** of any active filter designed using this op-amp.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Order Active Low Pass Filter (LPF) — Architecture

A **First-Order Active LPF** is constructed using a **passive RC low-pass network** at the input and an **op-amp in non-inverting configuration** as the gain stage. The op-amp provides:
1. **Isolation** between the source and the load (buffering)
2. **Adjustable passband gain** $A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$

### Circuit Topology (Conceptual Block)

$$
\text{Input} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{RC Network} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Non-Inverting Op-Amp} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Output}
$$

The RC low-pass section acts as a **voltage divider** whose division ratio is **frequency-dependent**. At low frequencies, the capacitor impedance is very high, so the full input appears at the non-inverting terminal. At high frequencies, the capacitor's impedance becomes negligible, shunting the signal to ground.

## 2.2 First-Order Active High Pass Filter (HPF) — Architecture

A **First-Order Active HPF** is the dual of the LPF: the **R and C positions are swapped** in the passive network. The high-pass RC network feeds the non-inverting terminal of the op-amp.

- At low frequencies ($f \to 0$): Capacitor impedance $\to \infty$, blocking the signal.
- At high frequencies ($f \to \infty$): Capacitor impedance $\to 0$, passing the signal.

## 2.3 Magnitude and Phase Response

| Parameter | Low Pass Filter | High Pass Filter |
|:---------:|:---------------:|:----------------:|
| Passband | $f \ll f_c$ | $f \gg f_c$ |
| Stopband | $f \gg f_c$ | $f \ll f_c$ |
| Phase at $f_c$ | $-45°$ | $+45°$ |
| Roll-off Rate | **−20 dB/decade** | **−20 dB/decade** |
| Attenuation at $2f_c$ | **−6 dB** | **−6 dB** |
| Attenuation at $10f_c$ | **−20 dB** | **−20 dB** |

> [!NOTE]
> **−20 dB/decade Roll-off:** A **first-order** filter attenuates the signal by a factor of 10 in voltage for every decade (10×) increase in frequency beyond $f_c$. This is equivalent to a slope of **−6 dB/octave** (2× frequency).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:---------:|:-------:|:-----------:|:-----:|
| 1 | Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Opposition to AC current | Ω |
| 2 | Cutoff Frequency | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | −3 dB frequency | Hz |
| 3 | LPF Transfer Function | $H(j\omega) = \frac{-A_0}{1 + j(\omega/\omega_c)}$ | Inverting config | Unitless |
| 4 | HPF Transfer Function | $H(j\omega) = \frac{-A_0 \, j(\omega/\omega_c)}{1 + j(\omega/\omega_c)}$ | Inverting config | Unitless |
| 5 | Passband Gain (Non-Inverting) | $A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$ | DC gain of LPF | V/V |
| 6 | Passband Gain (Inverting) | $A_0 = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | DC gain of HPF | V/V |
| 7 | Magnitude (dB) | $\vert H \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert H \vert$ | Bode magnitude | dB |
| 8 | LPF Magnitude Response | $\vert H \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$ | Bode plot eq. | V/V |
| 9 | HPF Magnitude Response | $\vert H \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f_c/f)^2}}$ | Bode plot eq. | V/V |
| 10 | Time Constant | $\tau = R C$ | RC product | seconds |
| 11 | Cutoff Angular Frequency | $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c = \frac{1}{R C}$ | radian frequency | rad/s |
| 12 | Attenuation Beyond $f_c$ | $-20 \log\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)$ dB | Stopband | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** When designing for KTU lab work, choose $C$ first in the range **$0.01\,\mu F$ to $0.1\,\mu F$** (standard E12 values), then compute $R$ from $R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$ to fall within the **$1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $100\,\text{k}\Omega$** range — this minimizes offset effects and keeps resistor noise low.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Active filters find critical applications in:
- **Audio Systems:** Crossover networks in speakers (woofer/tweeter filtering)
- **Communication Receivers:** Channel selection, anti-aliasing before ADC
- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG/EEG signal conditioning (removing 50 Hz mains hum)
- **Control Systems:** Lead-lag compensators in servo loops
- **Instrumentation:** Lock-in amplifiers, phase-sensitive detectors
- **Power Electronics:** Harmonic filtering in inverters (higher-order filters)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In KTU valuation, students often lose marks by confusing the **−3 dB point** with the **half-power point**. They are the same! At $f_c$, the output power is **half** the passband power ($P = V^2/R$, and $V$ drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Active Low Pass Filter (Inverting Configuration)

### Step 1 — Circuit Setup
Consider an **inverting op-amp configuration** where the input signal $V_{in}$ is applied through resistor $R_1$ to the inverting node, with **capacitor $C$** in the feedback path (parallel with feedback resistor $R_f$).

**Impedances in the s-domain:**
- Input branch impedance: $Z_{in} = R_1$
- Feedback branch impedance: $Z_f = R_f \parallel \frac{1}{sC}$

### Step 2 — Express Feedback Impedance

$$
Z_f = \frac{R_f \cdot \frac{1}{sC}}{R_f + \frac{1}{sC}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + s R_f C}
$$

**Explanation:** We applied the **parallel combination formula** $Z = \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ with $Z_1 = R_f$ and $Z_2 = \frac{1}{sC}$, then multiplied numerator and denominator by $sC$ to eliminate the fraction.

### Step 3 — Apply Inverting Amplifier Gain Formula

For an inverting op-amp, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_{in}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$
H(s) = -\frac{1}{R_1} \cdot \frac{R_f}{1 + s R_f C}
$$

$$
H(s) = -\frac{R_f / R_1}{1 + s R_f C}
$$

**Explanation:** We substituted $Z_f$ and $Z_{in}$ into the inverting gain equation. The result is a **first-order low-pass response** with DC gain $|H(0)| = R_f/R_1$.

### Step 5 — Identify Cutoff Frequency and Passband Gain

Comparing with the **standard first-order LPF form** $H(s) = \frac{-A_0}{1 + s/\omega_c}$:

$$
\boxed{A_0 = \frac{R_f}{R_1}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{R_f C} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}}
$$

### Step 6 — Magnitude Response for Bode Plot

Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$
H(j\omega) = \frac{-A_0}{1 + j(\omega / \omega_c)}
$$

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega / \omega_c)^2}} = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f / f_c)^2}}
$$

**Limiting cases:**
- When $f \ll f_c$: $\vert H \vert \to A_0$ (passband, flat)
- When $f = f_c$: $\vert H \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \, A_0$ (−3 dB point)
- When $f \gg f_c$: $\vert H \vert \to \frac{A_0}{f/f_c}$ (rolls off at −20 dB/decade)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: First-Order Active High Pass Filter (Inverting Configuration)

### Step 1 — Circuit Setup
Swap the positions: capacitor $C$ is now in **series with $R_{in}$** in the input branch, and **$R_f$ is the feedback element** alone.

**Impedances in the s-domain:**
- Input branch: $Z_{in} = R_{in} + \frac{1}{sC}$
- Feedback branch: $Z_f = R_f$

### Step 2 — Apply Inverting Amplifier Formula

$$
H(s) = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in} + \frac{1}{sC}}
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $sC$

$$
H(s) = -\frac{R_f \cdot sC}{s C R_{in} + 1}
$$

$$
H(s) = -\frac{s R_f C}{1 + s R_{in} C}
$$

**Explanation:** This algebraic step places the equation in standard form. The factor $s$ in the numerator indicates **high-pass behavior** (output is zero at DC, $s = 0$).

### Step 4 — Factor to Reveal Standard Form

$$
H(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} \cdot \frac{s R_{in} C}{1 + s R_{in} C}
$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$
H(j\omega) = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} \cdot \frac{j(\omega / \omega_c)}{1 + j(\omega / \omega_c)}
$$

### Step 5 — Identify Cutoff Frequency and Passband Gain

$$
\boxed{A_0 = \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in} C}}
$$

### Step 6 — Magnitude Response

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{A_0 \, (f / f_c)}{\sqrt{1 + (f / f_c)^2}} = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f_c / f)^2}}
$$

**Limiting cases:**
- When $f \ll f_c$: $\vert H \vert \to A_0 \cdot (f/f_c) \to 0$ (high attenuation)
- When $f = f_c$: $\vert H \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$ (−3 dB)
- When $f \gg f_c$: $\vert H \vert \to A_0$ (passband, flat)

---

## 3.3 Worked Design Example (KTU Lab Style)

**Design Specification:** Design a first-order active LPF with $f_c = 1$ kHz and passband gain $A_0 = 2$.

**Step 1:** Choose capacitor. Let $C = 0.01\,\mu F = 10\,\text{nF}$.

**Step 2:** Calculate resistor from cutoff formula.

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 15.915\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3:** Choose nearest standard E12 value $\rightarrow R \approx 15\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 4:** For non-inverting config with $A_0 = 1 + R_f/R_1 = 2$, we need $R_f = R_1$. Pick $R_f = R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 5:** Verify recalculated $f_c$ with chosen components.

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 15000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1061\,\text{Hz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Tolerance:** $\pm 5\%$ deviation is acceptable in KTU lab work. Document this in your lab record as a deliberate design choice.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Feature | Active LPF | Active HPF |
|:-------:|:----------:|:----------:|
| Op-Amp Configuration | Inverting / Non-inverting | Inverting / Non-inverting |
| Passive Network | $R$ then $C$ to ground | $C$ then $R$ to ground |
| DC Response | Passes DC | Blocks DC |
| High-Freq Response | Attenuates | Passes |
| Transfer Function | $\frac{-A_0}{1 + sRC}$ | $\frac{-A_0 sRC}{1 + sRC}$ |
| Phase Shift | $0°$ to $-90°$ | $+90°$ to $0°$ |
| Slope (Roll-off) | −20 dB/dec | −20 dB/dec |
| Pole Location | $-\omega_c$ on real axis | $-\omega_c$ on real axis |
| Zero Location | At $s = \infty$ | At $s = 0$ (origin) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — First-Order Active Low Pass Filter (Inverting)

```mermaid
graph LR
  A[Vin Source]:::input --> R1[Resistor R1]:::r
  R1 --> N1((Inverting Node)):::node
  N1 --> Cf[Capacitor Cf]:::c
  Cf --> OUT[Vout]:::output
  N1 --> Rf[Resistor Rf]:::r
  Rf --> OUT
  N1 --> GND1[Non-Inverting Input Tied to GND]:::gnd
  OUT --> GND2[Output Reference]:::gnd
  classDef input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
  classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
  classDef r fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
  classDef c fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000
  classDef node fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
  classDef gnd fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,color:#000
```

> **Reading the Schematic:** Input $V_{in}$ flows through $R_1$ into the inverting node. The feedback path contains $R_f$ **in parallel with** $C_f$. As frequency rises, $C_f$'s impedance drops, reducing the feedback impedance and thus the closed-loop gain — producing the low-pass action.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — First-Order Active High Pass Filter (Inverting)

```mermaid
graph LR
  A[Vin Source]:::input --> Cin[Capacitor Cin]:::c
  Cin --> R2[Resistor Rin]:::r
  R2 --> N1((Inverting Node)):::node
  N1 --> Rf2[Resistor Rf]:::r
  Rf2 --> OUT[Vout]:::output
  N1 --> GND1[Non-Inverting Input Tied to GND]:::gnd
  OUT --> GND2[Output Reference]:::gnd
  classDef input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
  classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
  classDef r fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
  classDef c fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000
  classDef node fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
  classDef gnd fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,color:#000
```

> **Reading the Schematic:** The capacitor $C_{in}$ is in **series** at the input. At low frequencies, $C_{in}$ presents high impedance, blocking the signal. At high frequencies, $C_{in}$ becomes a short, allowing the signal through $R_{in}$ to the inverting node. Feedback is via $R_f$ alone.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Frequency Response Behavior

```mermaid
graph TD
  subgraph LOW_FREQ_REGION["LOW FREQUENCY REGION"]
    LF1[F less than fc] --> LF2[Capacitor OPEN Circuit] --> LF3[Signal BLOCKED in HPF]
    LF3A[F less than fc] --> LF3B[Capacitor HIGH Z] --> LF3C[Signal PASSED in LPF]
  end
  subgraph HIGH_FREQ_REGION["HIGH FREQUENCY REGION"]
    HF1[F greater than fc] --> HF2[Capacitor SHORT Circuit] --> HF3[Signal PASSED in HPF]
    HF3A[F greater than fc] --> HF3B[Capacitor LOW Z] --> HF3C[Signal BLOCKED in LPF]
  end
  subgraph CUTOVER["AT CUTOFF FREQUENCY fc"]
    CO1[Output equals 0.707 of Input] --> CO2[Phase Shift 45 degrees] --> CO3[Power Drops to Half]
  end
  classDef block fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
  classDef pass fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
  classDef block2 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — KTU Lab Setup Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
  FG[Function Generator providing Sine Input 10Hz to 100kHz]:::src --> CK[CRO Channel 1 Monitoring Input]:::meas
  FG --> CB[Breadboard with Op-Amp 741 and RC Components]:::circuit
  CB --> CK2[CRO Channel 2 Monitoring Output]:::meas
  CB --> PWR[Plus Minus 15V DC Dual Power Supply]:::supply
  FG -->|Vary frequency| FSWEEP[Logarithmic Frequency Sweep 100Hz 1kHz 10kHz 100kHz]:::proc
  FSWEEP --> TAB[Tabulate Vin Vout and Compute Gain in dB]:::proc
  TAB --> PLOT[Plot Gain dB versus log f Bode Plot]:::proc
  PLOT --> FC[Identify 3dB Point as Cutoff Frequency]:::proc
  classDef src fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
  classDef circuit fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Connection Tip:** Pin configuration of IC 741: Pin 2 = Inverting Input, Pin 3 = Non-Inverting Input, Pin 6 = Output, Pin 7 = +V, Pin 4 = −V, Pin 1 & Pin 5 = Offset Null. **Always use decoupling capacitors (0.1 µF) across the power supply pins** to suppress noise.
